title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: Combustion and Flame 151 (2007) 274–288 www.elsevier.com/locate/combustﬂame Effect of pressure and fuel–air unmixedness on NOx emissions from industrial gas turbine burners Fernando Biagioli ∗, Felix Güthe ALSTOM Switzerland, Brown Boveri Strasse 7, Baden, CH-5401, Switzerland Received 23 November 2006; received in revised form 5 April 2007; accepted 25 April 2007 Available online 11 June 2007 Abstract The effect of fuel–air unmixedness on NOx emissions from industrial lean premixed gas turbine burners fueled with natural gas is analyzed in the pressure range from 1 to 30 bar. The analysis is based on a model where NOx production is split, according to a Damköhler number criterion, into a “prompt” (fast) contribution generated within the very narrow instantaneous heat release region (ﬂamelet) and a “postﬂame” (slow) one, generated in the combustion products. Using GRI3.0 chemical kinetics, it is found that (a) the prompt NOx contribution is approximately a factor of 3 less sensitive to adiabatic ﬂame temperature variations than postﬂame NOx and (b) prompt and postﬂame NOx change with pressure respectively according to an exponent αPR ≃−0.45 and αPF ≃ 0.67. It is shown that total NOx emissions change from being mostly of prompt type at 1 bar to being mostly of postﬂame type at 30 bar, so that the effect of fuel–air unmixedness on NOx emissions signiﬁcantly increases with increasing pressure. The combination of these ﬁndings yields a negative NOx pressure exponent under fully premixed conditions across a rather large range of equivalence ratios but a positive one for levels of fuel–air

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: type at 30 bar, so that the effect of fuel–air unmixedness on NOx emissions signiﬁcantly increases with increasing pressure. The combination of these ﬁndings yields a negative NOx pressure exponent under fully premixed conditions across a rather large range of equivalence ratios but a positive one for levels of fuel–air unmixedness typical of industrial burners. This result is conﬁrmed by the application of the NOx model in the large eddy simulation of the ALSTOM EV double cone burner, which gives, in line with experimental data, an NOx pressure exponent growing, with equivalence ratio, from ≃0.1 to ≃0.67. © 2007 The Combustion Institute. Introduction Developing lean premixed industrial gas turbine burners is a complex and expensive task. Additionally to high pressure testing, in order to keep development costs to a reasonable level, gas turbine manufactur- ers also make signiﬁcant use of atmospheric tests. A very common assumption for NOx pres- sure scaling is the ≃p0.5 dependency [2]. In this case, extrapolation from 1 bar to high pressure would be possible on the basis of just a universal pressure scal- ing exponent. Other investigators have, however, re- ported a more complicated situation, sometimes even with a negative pressure exponent dependency (NOx decreasing with pressure). For

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: ≃p0.5 dependency [2]. In this case, extrapolation from 1 bar to high pressure would be possible on the basis of just a universal pressure scal- ing exponent. Other investigators have, however, re- ported a more complicated situation, sometimes even with a negative pressure exponent dependency (NOx decreasing with pressure). For example, a reduction of NOx emissions with pressure has been observed in the experiments performed in [3–5]. The experi- ments in a high-pressure jet-stirred reactor performed in [3] have shown that, at a combustion tempera- ture of 1800 K and a residence time around 2 ms, NOx emissions decrease with pressure and this re- duction is more pronounced in the range up to 5 bar than in the range above it. Similar NOx behavior was observed in the experiments performed in [4,5] for the same kind of burner, with [5] reporting a neg- ative pressure scaling exponent equal to −0.2. In a review paper [6], it was suggested that NOx emis- sions scale with pressure ([NOx]/[NOx]1 bar = pα) with an exponent α that increases from α = 0 under very lean premixed conditions to α = 0.5 for higher equivalence ratios. It was instead concluded in [7], on the basis of experiments, that NOx emissions in well premixed ﬂames are independent of pressure and suggested that, in well-designed lean premixed burn- ers, it is possible to operate a combustor from 1 to 30 bar without any signiﬁcant change in NOx emis- sions. In these experiments the pressure dependency was

text: basis of experiments, that NOx emissions in well premixed ﬂames are independent of pressure and suggested that, in well-designed lean premixed burn- ers, it is possible to operate a combustor from 1 to 30 bar without any signiﬁcant change in NOx emis- sions. In these experiments the pressure dependency was found to increase only slightly with increasing equivalence ratio. This conclusion was also reached in [8], where experiments indicated that NOx emis- sions are independent of pressure at an equivalence ratio of 0.5 but that pressure dependency increases as soon as the fuel–air unmixedness level is increased. Given these results from previous experimental inves- tigations, the primary scope of the present work is to shed additional light on the combined effect of pres- 276 F. Biagioli, F. Güthe / Combustion and Flame 151 (2007) 274–288 sure and unmixedness on NOx emissions, especially from industrial gas turbine burners. Turbulent combustion in industrial lean premixed burners takes place at high Reynolds and, for fuels like natural gas, moderately high Damköhler (Da) numbers. Under these conditions, heat is released in regions that are much smaller than the integral length scale of turbulence; i.e., combustion takes place in the so-called ﬂamelet regime. However, typical values of the Karlovitz (Ka) number, i.e., the ratio between the heat release and the Kolmogorov time scales, are usually still larger than unity, meaning that the small- est turbulent eddies can penetrate the preheating re- gion, which therefore becomes subject to an increase of thickness until a new equilibrium

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: values of the Karlovitz (Ka) number, i.e., the ratio between the heat release and the Kolmogorov time scales, are usually still larger than unity, meaning that the small- est turbulent eddies can penetrate the preheating re- gion, which therefore becomes subject to an increase of thickness until a new equilibrium between turbu- lent internal diffusion and heat release is reached. The problem of turbulent premixed combustion un- der Da > 1, Ka > 1 conditions was ﬁrst investigated in [11] where the ﬁnal size δF of the ﬂamelet broad- ened by small-scale turbulence (from which comes the name of thickened ﬂamelet combustion regime) was estimated from the turbulent diffusion law as δF = Dt,FτHR, where Dt,F is the diffusion coef- ﬁcient due to vortices of size equal and smaller to the ﬂamelet thickness δF, i.e., Dt,F ≃u′ FδF. The tur- bulence intensity associated with these vortices can be estimated from the equilibrium of turbulence ki- netic energy ﬂux across the turbulent spectrum (i.e., u′3/lt = u′3 k /lk) as u′ F ≃u′(δF/lt)1/3, which, used within the expression for δF, gives, after some alge- braic manipulation, the ﬁnal value of δF ≃ltDa−3/2. This expression shows that the thickened ﬂamelet has a size that is a Da−3/2 fraction of the integral length scale of turbulence. In the case of Da > 1, small-scale turbulence with size larger than δF has the effect of wrinkling the ﬂamelet, increasing its area and there- fore the mean total burning rate. For consistency with the above

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: size that is a Da−3/2 fraction of the integral length scale of turbulence. In the case of Da > 1, small-scale turbulence with size larger than δF has the effect of wrinkling the ﬂamelet, increasing its area and there- fore the mean total burning rate. For consistency with the above ﬂamelet structure of the preheating and heat release regions, NOx for- mation is split in this work into two contributions ac- cording to a Damköhler number criterion: a fast one (called here “prompt”), which is the NOx part formed within the ﬂamelet region, and a slow-forming part (called here “postﬂame”), which takes place in the hot gases downstream of the instantaneous heat release region. A detailed study of the turbulence–chemistry interaction effects inside the thickened ﬂamelet struc- ture would be too demanding for the scope of the present investigation. The analysis has therefore been carried out assuming an instantaneous turbulent pre- mixed ﬂamelet with a structure like that of a lam- inar freely propagating ﬂame. This assumption is partially justiﬁed by the fact that combustion reac- tions are characterized by high activation energy. In this case the thickness of the heat-release region is much smaller than the thickness of the preheating one, this last, for Da > 1, Ka > 1, broadened by small-scale turbulence. It can therefore be reason- ably assumed that the instantaneous-heat-release re- gion, where prompt NOx formation takes place, has a size smaller than that of the Kolmogorov vortices and therefore remains laminar. The strain effect

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: preheating one, this last, for Da > 1, Ka > 1, broadened by small-scale turbulence. It can therefore be reason- ably assumed that the instantaneous-heat-release re- gion, where prompt NOx formation takes place, has a size smaller than that of the Kolmogorov vortices and therefore remains laminar. The strain effect pro- duced by the Kolmogorov vortices on the laminar heat release region is also neglected. This could eventu- ally be accounted for replacing the freely propagating ﬂame structure with one of counterﬂowing laminar ﬂames where the strain rate is an independent para- meter to match to the Kolmogorov strain rate. The present work is organized as follows. In Section 2, NOx formation is analyzed under typi- cal conditions of gas turbine combustors and in the case of fully premixed reactants, with the CHEMKIN II–PREMIX module [12] and GRI3.0 [13] chemi- cal kinetics. The analysis is supported via experi- mental data in a fully premixed, strongly adiabatic dump combustor that was already used in previ- ous experimental investigations of turbulent premixed ﬂames [14]. In Section 3, a computational model to account for the combined effect of unmixedness and pressure on NOx emissions is developed. The model is developed for CFD simulations of industrial low-NOx burners. In this section the model is used according to a simple 1D formulation in order to obtain a preliminary idea of the effect of unmixedness and pressure on NOx emissions from industrial gas turbine burners. In Section 4, the NOx model is used in a large

NOx formation is split here into two contri- butions according to a Damköhler number criterion: a fast one (called here “prompt”) that is the NOx part formed within the heat-release region and a slow- forming part (called here “postﬂame”) that takes place in the hot gases downstream of the instantaneous- heat-release region. This decomposition of NOx formation is dictated by the idea that turbulence– chemistry interaction effects must be modeled consis- tently with their characteristic Damköhler numbers. Each species that is (promptly) produced or consumed within the ﬂamelet, from initial mass fraction Yj,u to ﬁnal value Yj,b, is released by the mean ﬂame el- ement of area dA0 at a rate (mass/time) given by ρuUt(Yj,b −Yj,u)dA0. As mentioned already in the Introduction, prompt NOx emissions are determined assuming a ﬂamelet sheet with structure equal to that of the freely prop- agating laminar ﬂame. Prompt NOx is therefore de- ﬁned as the NOx concentration in the laminar freely propagating ﬂame at the location where combustion is close to equilibrium, i.e., where the progress variable c (giving the degree of conversion of the fuel–air mix- ture into its products) is sufﬁciently large so that heat release has almost completed. A prompt NOx cutoff value for the progress variable equal to unity would, however, be inadequate because it includes also the full region of combustion products, where also the postﬂame NOx contribution is produced. A suitable value of the progress variable to detect the level of prompt NOx has been determined here from the plot of NOx concentration across the laminar ﬂame ver- sus the reaction progress variable shown in Fig. 2 for two ﬂame temperatures and two values of the oper- ating pressure (0.97 < c < 1.0). The plot shows that for c > 0.99 the heat release is almost completed in all cases (all heat release rates are below 7% of their maximum value) and also that the prompt NOx level does not change

The NOx emissions estimated via the CHEMKIN II–PREMIX module are compared here with exper- imental data from burners where reactants are com- pletely premixed prior to combustion (zero unmixed- ness at the ﬂame). Three pressure levels have been considered: 1, 20, and 30 bar. The 1-bar experimental data are from a fully premixed ALSTOM EV double- cone burner that has in the past been the subject of several experimental [17] and analytical [18–20] in- vestigations, while the 20- and 30-bar are from a fully premixed, strongly adiabatic dump combustor with a characteristic residence time of ≃20 ms. This dump combustor, available to the ALSTOM Combustion development, is based on a modular design consist- ing of an electric heating unit, a mixing tube that ends in a sudden area expansion in a cylindrical combus- tor, and a cooling/exhaust pipe. Three 30-kW electric heaters preheat the air stream to the desired tempera- ture (0–650 ◦C). A 30-cm-long series of static mixers allow high levels of fuel/air premixing before the re- actants reach the dump combustor. The mixing tube and the combustor are lined with ceramic insulation to promote near-adiabatic operation. Tests can be run with a maximum mass ﬂow rate of 250 g/s. The

text: ◦C). A 30-cm-long series of static mixers allow high levels of fuel/air premixing before the re- actants reach the dump combustor. The mixing tube and the combustor are lined with ceramic insulation to promote near-adiabatic operation. Tests can be run with a maximum mass ﬂow rate of 250 g/s. The max- imum achievable pressure is 35 bar. Full details of this experimental setup can be found in [14]. It must be noticed that, under perfect fuel–air pre- mixing and adiabatic conditions, the only relevant parameter in the formation of postﬂame NOx is the product residence time. The combustor, in fact, acts as a plug ﬂow reactor where the reacting volume V is bounded by the ﬂame on one side and the combustor exit on the other. Products ﬂow through this volume with a volumetric ﬂow rate F such that the ﬁnal res- idence time is V/F . The volume where prompt NOx formation (and heat release) takes place is considered here small with respect to the hot gas postﬂame vol- ume, consistent with the hypothesis of ﬂamelet com- bustion. This hypothesis loses validity in the very- low-ﬂame-temperature range, which, however, is not of large interest for gas turbines. The comparison between calculated and measured NOx emissions is shown in Fig. 5. Postﬂame NOx is calculated for a residence time of 18 ms, approx- imately the residence time in the combustors. The right part of the ﬁgure shows a comparison of experi- mental NOx emissions with the prompt and postﬂame contributions

When added together, prompt and postﬂame NOx yield a total NOx pressure exponent that goes from a negative value of ≃−0.4 at TREF/T = 1.07 to a positive value of ≃0.6 at TREF/T = 0.94. In the low-temperature range, in fact, postﬂame plus prompt NOx at high pressure is much lower than the prompt NOx contribution dominating total NOx emissions at 1 bar. Postﬂame NOx, however, rises with temper- ature much more quickly than prompt NOx (due to higher activation temperature), so that the situation is reversed at TREF/T ≃0.99, where the high-pressure postﬂame contribution becomes equal to the 1-bar prompt one. Finally, at TREF/T = 0.94, the postﬂame high-pressure NOx contribution dominates above the 1-bar total NOx, giving a positive pressure exponent of ≃0.6. The characteristic temperature at which the 1- and 30-bar total NOx emissions become equal decreases with increasing residence time. Modeling The experimental data presented in the previous section for the case of perfectly premixed ﬂames indi- cate that the sensitivity of NOx emissions to temper- ature increases with pressure. It has been shown that NOx emissions behave in this way due to the increase in the postﬂame NOx contribution and decrease of prompt (ﬂamelet) NOx

text: the previous section for the case of perfectly premixed ﬂames indi- cate that the sensitivity of NOx emissions to temper- ature increases with pressure. It has been shown that NOx emissions behave in this way due to the increase in the postﬂame NOx contribution and decrease of prompt (ﬂamelet) NOx with increasing pressure, the former much more sensitive to temperature variations than the latter. This result suggests also that, the higher the pres- sure, the higher should be the effect of fuel–air un- mixedness on NOx emissions. In this section a CFD model able to account for postﬂame and prompt NOx under partially premixed conditions is developed. The model is therefore applied here in a simple one- dimensional form in order to get a preliminary as- sessment on how fuel–air unmixedness and pressure affect NOx emissions. In industrial burners, some degree of unmixedness (partially premixed reactants) at the ﬂame is usually necessary in order to have good ﬂame stabilization properties. Under partially premixed conditions, the estimation of NOx emissions becomes a more com- plicated issue. It is assumed here that, also in pres- ence of partial premixing, the ﬂamelet sheet preserves the structure of a 1D unstrained freely propagating laminar ﬂame at the instantaneous local value of the equivalence ratio. In this case, the equivalence ratio at the ﬂamelet sheet and thus also the concentration of released prompt NOx are functions of time and space. This situation is accounted for with a model de- scribing the release of prompt NOx from the ﬂamelet surface with concentration depending on the local in- stantaneous equivalence ratio and then the successive downstream micromixing of these concentrations. The model developed here for prompt NOx orig- inates from the fact that molecular mixing is a lin- ear process, while the distribution of prompt NOx released from the ﬂamelet (see Fig. 7, left) with con- centration n⋆ PR(f ) from CHEMKIN II–PREMIX cal- culations is nonlinear (Fig. 7, right). The ﬁnal con- centration of prompt NOx at the outlet of the combus- tor, where complete fuel–air premixing is achieved, is therefore different from the one obtained from a 1D premixed ﬂame

The effect of unmixedness on the ﬁnal prompt NOx concentration at the combustor exit is here ac- counted for using n⋆ PR(f ) as a boundary condition on the ﬂuctuating instantaneous ﬂamelet sheet for a conservation equation that describes the downstream micromixing of prompt NOx. Extending this idea to a Reynolds or LES (large eddy simulation) Navier–Stokes averaging frame- work, however, implies some difﬁculties. (10) and (14) is applied in a simple one-dimensional form in order to make a preliminary assessment of the effect of unmixed- ness and pressure on NOx emissions. In gas turbine combustors, fuel–air unmixedness decreases rapidly on moving from inside the burner toward the combus- tor. Average hot gas temperatures are also subject to a fast drop in the combustor due to heat losses and mixing with liner cooling air. For these reasons NOx production rates are signiﬁcant only in the burner and in a small hot core in front of it. In this simple analy- sis, it is assumed that fuel–air unmixedness decays over a characteristic time τm that is comparable to the residence time in the hot core just in front of the exit of the burner. In the case of the EV burner variant con- sidered here, this time is on the order of 5–10 ms. The simple analysis performed here gives the op- portunity to justify on a qualitative basis different NOx pressure exponents reported in the literature. A detailed evaluation of the NOx emissions from the experimental devices used in those investigations is outside the scope of the present work. The present work, however, suggests that the difference in the measured NOx pressure exponents is due to the par- ticular combination of residence times, equivalence ratio, and unmixedness effects characterizing those devices. For example, the experiments performed in a jet-stirred perfectly premixed reactor cavity over residence times up to 4 ms in [3,4] gave a signif- icant reduction of NOx emissions with pressure, in line with the present simple analysis under the same conditions. Higher levels of unmixedness and/or res- idence times might have instead characterized those experiments where a positive pressure exponent was detected (e.g., [8]). 4. CFD analysis of NOx emissions in the ALSTOM EV double cone burner Industrial dry low-NOx burners usually stabilize the ﬂame at a central recirculation zone (CRZ) ob- tained via the phenomenon of vortex breakdown. This phenomenon is brieﬂy explained here for the case of the ALSTOM EnVironmental (EV) double cone burner sketched in Fig. 9. This burner, which has been the subject of previous experimental [17] and compu- tational investigations [18–20], is obtained by shifting two halves of a cone in opposite directions, orthogo- nal to the cone axis, so that two inlet slots of constant width are produced. The air stream, when ﬂowing through the burner slots, is given a strong swirl com- ponent. At the same time, fuel is injected through holes uniformly distributed along the burner slots, or- thogonal to the stream of air. This ensures that all air streamlines through the slots are equally fed with fuel; i.e., it ensures good (but not perfect) fuel–air pre- mixing prior to combustion. When the swirling ﬂow reaches the expansion area between burner and com- bustor, the ﬂuid particles radially diverge and slow down in the azimuthal direction due to the constraint imposed by the conservation of circulation Γ = wr. The vorticity vector ω rotates with the streamlines in the azimuthal direction so that part of the axial vortic- ity is converted to azimuthal vorticity, which, accord- ing to the

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: and slow down in the azimuthal direction due to the constraint imposed by the conservation of circulation Γ = wr. The vorticity vector ω rotates with the streamlines in the azimuthal direction so that part of the axial vortic- ity is converted to azimuthal vorticity, which, accord- ing to the Biot–Savart law, induces a negative axial velocity at the axis [25]. If the negative axial veloc- ity induced at the axis is sufﬁciently large, breakdown of the axial vortex takes place and a CRZ at the axis is created. The ﬂame anchors at the axis, in proximity F. Center: experimental data. Right: LES model. to the front stagnation point of the CRZ, where axial convective velocities are sufﬁciently low. From a pure NOx emissions point of view, the op- timal situation is that reactants reach the ﬂame com- pletely premixed. However, during the burner devel- opment process, fuel–air unmixedness at the ﬂame is used as an additional degree of freedom that helps to provide good ﬂame-anchoring properties, i.e., suf- ﬁciently high margin from lean blow out and low- pressure pulsations due to thermoacoustic combustion instabilities (these are notoriously related to ﬂame po- sition by fuel injection–ﬂame time lag effects). Fuel injection is therefore optimized for emissions and ﬂame stability simultaneously; i.e., industrial “pre- mixed” burners are in general always

text: ﬁciently high margin from lean blow out and low- pressure pulsations due to thermoacoustic combustion instabilities (these are notoriously related to ﬂame po- sition by fuel injection–ﬂame time lag effects). Fuel injection is therefore optimized for emissions and ﬂame stability simultaneously; i.e., industrial “pre- mixed” burners are in general always characterized by some residual fuel–air unmixedness at the ﬂame. The CFD model for prompt and postﬂame NOx presented in the previous section is applied here to the ALSTOM EV double-cone burner. All CFD cal- culations have been performed using a commercial unstructured CFD ﬁnite-volumes solver (FLUENT version 6.2.16). All geometric quantities and veloci- ties are nondimensionalized with the burner diameter D and burner bulk velocity U0. The combustor has a circular cross section with nondimensional diame- ter and length respectively equal to 2 and 7. To im- prove numerical accuracy and overcome difﬁculties connected with the complexity of the geometry, the computational domain has been meshed with a mixed tetrahedral–hexahedral mesh of approx. 400,000 ele- ments, with the hexahedrals concentrated in the cen- tral region of the burner and in the combustor. The ﬁnite-volume elements inside the burner and in the combustor close to the burner exit have an average nondimensional size of 0.025. A longitudinal cross section of the burner–combustor domain showing the computational mesh is presented in Fig. 9. The RANS calculations have been run with the realizable version [26] of the k–ϵ transport model and with numerics based on the QUICK scheme [27]. The LES simula- tions

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: nondimensional size of 0.025. A longitudinal cross section of the burner–combustor domain showing the computational mesh is presented in Fig. 9. The RANS calculations have been run with the realizable version [26] of the k–ϵ transport model and with numerics based on the QUICK scheme [27]. The LES simula- tions are based instead on the standard Smagorinsky subgrid model, with central differencing used for all variables with the exception of the transport equation for the fuel mixture fraction, which is discretized with the QUICK scheme [27]. All LES simulations have been run using second-order discretization in time and a nondimensional time step t/(D/U0) = 0.014. The calculations have been performed on four nodes of a LINUX cluster. The LES calculations have ﬁrst been run for a time sufﬁciently long to yield a statistically steady solution. Final averaging has then been per- formed over a total time t/(D/U0) ≃10, i.e., approx 700 time steps, which required around 12 h of CPU time for each case. The combusting ﬂow is calculated using the TFC combustion model according to its standard formula- tion based on Eq. (15). In the case of the LES cal- culations, the model constant A has been taken equal to 0.9, higher than the standard RANS value of 0.5. A discussion explaining the reasons for this higher value of the constant in LES simulations is given in [20]. Previous work [20] has shown that RANS methods are not suitable for correctly reproducing the ﬂame anchoring properties of this burner.

Previous work [20] has shown that RANS methods are not suitable for correctly reproducing the ﬂame anchoring properties of this burner. It was in fact found that RANS methods based on the k–ϵ transport model do not correctly reproduce the char- acteristics of the CRZ produced by the vortex break- down phenomenon, which are very important in de- termining the anchoring location of the turbulent pre- mixed ﬂame at the axis. Accurate results have instead been obtained via LES. This issue is documented in Fig. 10, which shows a comparison of the mean axial velocity ﬂowﬁeld obtained from the RANS and LES calculations with experimental data from a water rig facility. While the position of the apex of the CRZ is correctly predicted with the realizable k–ϵ method (deep in the burner), the radial size is not. Much bet- ter agreement with experiments, in terms of both the position and the radial size of the CRZ (see Fig. 10, right), is instead obtained via LES. It has been shown in [20] that the radial size of the CRZ has a strong effect on the anchoring posi- tion at the axis of the turbulent premixed ﬂame. LES has also been run under fully premixed con- ditions, in order to evaluate the theoretical NOx po- tential of the burner. The results, shown in Fig. 12 left for this case, indicate that, in the absence of fuel–air unmixedness and in line with the 1D analysis, high- pressure NOx is lower than atmospheric NOx over a rather large range of ﬂame temperature. Such low NOx emissions are, however, not easy to achieve in an industrial combustor due to the fact that a given level of fuel–air unmixedness is usually necessary to ensure ﬂame stability. In other words, the problem of achieving single-digit NOx emissions from industrial burners is a pure ﬂame stability one. 5. Conclusions The effect of pressure and fuel–air unmixedness on NOx emissions from industrial gas turbines burn- ers has been analyzed here using results from the CHEMKIN II premix module with GRI3.0 mecha- nisms and a model where the total NOx formation is decomposed, according to a Damköhler number crite- rion, to a fast “prompt” contribution produced within the heat release region and a slow “postﬂame” one. The analysis shows that (a) the ﬂamelet “prompt” contribution is around a factor of 3 less sensitive to fuel–air unmixedness than the “postﬂame” one, (b) the sensitivity to unmixedness of “prompt” and “postﬂame” NOx does not strongly depends on pres- sure, and (c) the “prompt” and “postﬂame” contri- butions change with pressure respectively with expo- nents ≃−0.45 and ≃0.67. A preliminary analysis has been carried out using a simple 1D approach and a model for NOx emis- sions developed here that separately accounts for the prompt and postﬂame contributions. Based on this analysis, the combination of the NOx emissions prop- erties from CHEMKIN II–GRI3.0 chemistry, under fully premixed conditions and typical hot gas resi- dence times in industrial burners, shows that high- pressure (e.g., 20-bar) NOx emissions can in principle be lower than atmospheric ones (negative pressure exponent of total NOx). Given, however, that post- ﬂame NOx represents the dominant contribution

title: Effect_of_pressure_and_fuel-air_unmixedness_on_NOx
text: from CHEMKIN II–GRI3.0 chemistry, under fully premixed conditions and typical hot gas resi- dence times in industrial burners, shows that high- pressure (e.g., 20-bar) NOx emissions can in principle be lower than atmospheric ones (negative pressure exponent of total NOx). Given, however, that post- ﬂame NOx represents the dominant contribution at high-pressure and given its highest sensitivity to ﬂame temperature variations, in the presence of even a small amount of fuel–air unmixedness or high residence times, the situation can easily be reversed in favor of atmospheric NOx emissions. The problem of reduc- ing NOx emissions to single-digit values in industrial gas turbines is therefore a problem of sorting out those ﬂame stability issues that imply keeping some degree of fuel–air unmixedness at the ﬂame. The results from the simple 1D analysis are ﬁnally conﬁrmed by LES simulations of the combusting ﬂow in the ALSTOM industrial EV burner. These, in line with experiments, give a positive NOx pressure ex- ponent increasing with equivalence ratio from ≃0.1 to ≃0.67 under standard fuel injection, but a nega- tive pressure exponent could be obtained over a large range of the equivalence ratio under ideal fully pre- mixed conditions.
